title: Nagios/Zabbix Alert Ingestion
spl:
index=monitoring sourcetype="nagios:notification" OR sourcetype="zabbix:webhook"
| stats count by host, service, state, severity
| sort -count
description: Consolidating legacy monitoring alerts into Splunk enables cross-tool correlation and single-pane-of-glass operations.
value: Consolidating legacy monitoring alerts into Splunk enables cross-tool correlation and single-pane-of-glass operations.
grandmaExplanation: Consolidating legacy monitoring alerts into our monitoring platform enables cross-tool correlation and single-pane-of-glass operations ‚Äî so you always trust the numbers you are looking at.
implementation: Configure Nagios/Zabbix to send alerts to Splunk via webhook or syslog. Normalize alert fields (host, service, severity, state) using CIM. Correlate with Splunk-native monitoring. Phase out legacy tools over time.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom webhook input, syslog.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: Nagios/Zabbix webhook exports, syslog notifications.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Configure Nagios/Zabbix to send alerts to Splunk via webhook or syslog. Normalize alert fields (host, service, severity, state) using CIM. Correlate with Splunk-native monitoring. Phase out legacy tools over time.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=monitoring sourcetype="nagios:notification" OR sourcetype="zabbix:webhook"
| stats count by host, service, state, severity
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Nagios/Zabbix Alert Ingestion** ‚Äî Consolidating legacy monitoring alerts into Splunk enables cross-tool correlation and single-pane-of-glass operations.

Documented **Data sources**: Nagios/Zabbix webhook exports, syslog notifications. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom webhook input, syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: monitoring; **sourcetype**: nagios:notification, zabbix:webhook. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=monitoring, sourcetype="nagios:notification". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, service, state, severity** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (third-party alerts), Bar chart (alerts by source tool), Status grid (host √ó service).
visualization: Table (third-party alerts), Bar chart (alerts by source tool), Status grid (host √ó service).
